task: Produce a STRIDE threat model for a mobile banking app with biometric auth, P2P payments, and third-party integrations, including threat matrix, risk ratings, and prioritized mitigations

criteria.description: Expert-level threat identification combining systematic STRIDE methodology with deep financial services threat intelligence, covering mobile banking attack vectors across all threat categories while emphasizing domain-specific financial attack patterns
criteria.pass_condition: Minimum 20 distinct threats with at least 2 per STRIDE category, including minimum 12 FinServ-specific threats (3 SIM swapping vectors, 4 transaction fraud mechanisms, 2 biometric bypass techniques, 3 third-party integration vulnerabilities), covering all three application components with attack chain descriptions including entry point, methodology, required access/tools, and business impact

sub_attributes.description: Quantity and balanced distribution of threats across STRIDE categories
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if 20+ threats with 3+ per category, 0.7 if 15-19 threats with 2+ per category, 0.4 if 12-14 threats with gaps in categories, 0.0 if <12 threats

sub_attributes.description: Inclusion of mobile banking domain-specific threats that generic security practitioners would miss
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if 10+ domain-specific threats including SIM swapping, biometric spoofing, financial malware, ACH/wire fraud vectors, 0.7 if 6-9 domain threats, 0.4 if 3-5 generic mobile threats only, 0.0 if purely generic threats

sub_attributes.description: Threat coverage across biometric auth, P2P payments, and third-party integrations with attack chain analysis
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if all components covered with multi-step attack chains, 0.7 if all components with single-step threats, 0.4 if 2/3 components covered, 0.0 if <2 components

sub_attributes.description: Threats mapped to financial regulatory concerns (PCI-DSS, PSD2, FFIEC guidance)
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if threats explicitly reference regulatory frameworks with compliance mappings, 0.7 if implicit regulatory awareness, 0.4 if generic compliance mentions, 0.0 if no regulatory consideration

pass_examples: Spoofing: SIM swapping to bypass SMS 2FA, biometric template injection attacks, OAuth client impersonation; Tampering: Binary patching to bypass certificate pinning, transaction amount modification in transit
fail_examples: Generic threats like 'password guessing' and 'man-in-the-middle attacks' without mobile banking context or attack chain specificity
criteria.research_basis: NIST Cybersecurity Framework 2.0 emphasizes comprehensive threat identification, while OWASP Mobile Security Testing Guide requires domain-specific mobile threat enumeration for financial applications

criteria.description: Comprehensive analysis of mobile-specific attack surfaces including binary tampering, runtime manipulation, reverse engineering, and platform-specific vulnerabilities
criteria.pass_condition: Coverage of minimum 8 mobile-specific attack vectors including binary analysis, runtime manipulation, certificate pinning bypass, root/jailbreak detection evasion, and app cloning with descriptions of attack methodology, required tools/skills, and exploitation steps

sub_attributes.description: Analysis of application binary modification, code injection, and static analysis bypass techniques
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if details binary patching, code injection, and anti-tamper bypass with specific tools/techniques, 0.7 if covers basic binary modification threats, 0.4 if mentions app integrity generically, 0.0 if absent

sub_attributes.description: Dynamic analysis of runtime hooks, memory manipulation, and debugging attacks
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if analyzes Frida/Xposed hooking, memory dumping, runtime modification with technical detail, 0.7 if covers basic runtime attacks, 0.4 if mentions dynamic analysis threats, 0.0 if static analysis only

sub_attributes.description: iOS and Android specific security model bypasses and platform vulnerabilities
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if covers both platforms with specific vulnerability classes and bypass techniques, 0.7 if covers one platform thoroughly, 0.4 if basic platform awareness, 0.0 if platform agnostic only

sub_attributes.description: Analysis of certificate pinning bypass, root/jailbreak detection evasion, and anti-debugging circumvention
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if details multiple defense bypass techniques with implementation specifics, 0.7 if covers basic bypass methods, 0.4 if mentions defense evasion, 0.0 if assumes defenses are absolute

pass_examples: Binary tampering via APK/IPA modification to bypass certificate pinning using objection/Frida, runtime hooking of biometric APIs to extract templates
fail_examples: Generic 'malware installation' without specific mobile attack vectors or technical implementation details
criteria.research_basis: OWASP Mobile Application Security Testing Guide (MASTG) defines mobile-specific threat categories, while NIST SP 800-124 emphasizes mobile platform security considerations

criteria.description: Deep analysis of biometric implementation threats including template extraction, spoofing attacks, Secure Enclave vulnerabilities, and biometric lifecycle security
criteria.pass_condition: Analysis of minimum 6 biometric-specific threats including template extraction, presentation attacks, liveness detection bypass, secure storage vulnerabilities, and fallback mechanism abuse with descriptions of attack methodology and system components involved (hardware security modules, trusted execution environments, biometric sensors)

sub_attributes.description: Analysis of biometric template storage, extraction, and cryptographic protection mechanisms
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if analyzes TEE/Secure Enclave protection, template encryption, and extraction attack vectors with specific vulnerabilities, 0.7 if covers template security basics, 0.4 if mentions secure storage, 0.0 if treats templates as regular data

sub_attributes.description: Technical analysis of spoofing attacks, liveness detection bypass, and presentation attack detection evasion
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if details specific spoofing methods (photos, videos, 3D models) and liveness bypass techniques, 0.7 if covers basic presentation attacks, 0.4 if mentions spoofing generically, 0.0 if absent

sub_attributes.description: Analysis of fallback authentication mechanisms and their exploitation in biometric failure scenarios
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if analyzes fallback triggers, PIN/password bypass, and forced fallback attacks, 0.7 if identifies fallback weaknesses, 0.4 if mentions alternative auth, 0.0 if ignores fallback scenarios

sub_attributes.description: Assessment of HSM integration, TEE utilization, and hardware-backed biometric security
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if analyzes specific hardware security implementations and vulnerabilities, 0.7 if understands hardware security role, 0.4 if mentions hardware security, 0.0 if software-only focus

pass_examples: Template extraction via TEE vulnerability exploitation, presentation attack using high-resolution photos to bypass liveness detection, forced fallback to weak PIN authentication
fail_examples: Generic 'biometric spoofing' without technical detail on attack methods or security architecture understanding
criteria.research_basis: NIST SP 800-63B Digital Identity Guidelines for biometric authentication and Common Criteria evaluations for biometric template protection requirements

criteria.description: Comprehensive evaluation of API security, OAuth implementation, supply chain risks, and data sharing boundaries with external services
criteria.pass_condition: Analysis of minimum 8 integration-specific threats including OAuth token hijacking, API abuse, SDK vulnerabilities, supply chain compromise, and data leakage vectors with identification of vulnerable integration patterns and security boundary violations

sub_attributes.description: Technical analysis of OAuth 2.0/OIDC implementation vulnerabilities and token management
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if analyzes OAuth flows, token storage, PKCE implementation, and specific OAuth attack vectors, 0.7 if covers basic OAuth security, 0.4 if mentions token security, 0.0 if generic API security only

sub_attributes.description: Analysis of REST/GraphQL API vulnerabilities, rate limiting bypass, and injection attacks
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if details API-specific attack vectors with OWASP API Top 10 coverage, 0.7 if covers basic API security threats, 0.4 if mentions API vulnerabilities, 0.0 if absent

sub_attributes.description: Assessment of third-party SDK vulnerabilities, dependency risks, and vendor security posture
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if analyzes SDK vulnerabilities, dependency confusion, and vendor risk assessment methodologies, 0.7 if covers basic supply chain risks, 0.4 if mentions third-party risks, 0.0 if internal focus only

sub_attributes.description: Analysis of data exposure through integrations and privacy boundary violations
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if analyzes data minimization, consent boundaries, and integration data leakage vectors, 0.7 if covers data sharing risks, 0.4 if mentions data privacy, 0.0 if technical focus only

pass_examples: OAuth authorization code interception via deep link hijacking, SDK supply chain compromise through dependency confusion attacks, API data exfiltration via parameter pollution
fail_examples: Generic 'third-party risks' without specific technical attack vectors or integration architecture understanding
criteria.research_basis: OWASP API Security Top 10 and OAuth 2.1 Security Best Current Practice RFC emphasize integration-specific threat vectors

criteria.description: Financial services-appropriate risk rating methodology incorporating monetary impact thresholds, likelihood assessments, and regulatory impact considerations
criteria.pass_condition: Risk ratings using quantitative monetary impact categories (e.g., Low: <$10K, Medium: $10K-$100K, High: $100K-$1M, Critical: >$1M), regulatory penalty considerations, and operational impact metrics with at least 3 High/Critical rated threats justified by impact category assignment and likelihood assessment

sub_attributes.description: Use of specific dollar thresholds and financial impact calculations rather than generic risk scales
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if quantitative impact bands with specific dollar thresholds and loss calculations, 0.7 if monetary ranges without calculations, 0.4 if qualitative financial impact mentions, 0.0 if generic high/medium/low only

sub_attributes.description: Incorporation of regulatory penalties and compliance violations in risk calculations
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if specific regulatory penalties quantified (PCI fines, regulatory sanctions), 0.7 if regulatory impact mentioned in ratings, 0.4 if compliance mentioned without impact, 0.0 if no regulatory consideration

sub_attributes.description: Banking-specific operational impact including customer impact and business continuity metrics
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if specific operational metrics (transaction volume impact, customer churn, SLA breaches), 0.7 if operational impact categories defined, 0.4 if generic business impact, 0.0 if technical impact only

pass_examples: P2P payment tampering rated Critical: potential $500K+ daily fraud exposure, PCI violation penalties $50K-$500K, 15% customer churn from trust loss
fail_examples: Threats rated 'High' based on generic CVSS scores without consideration of financial loss thresholds or banking-specific operational impact
criteria.research_basis: FFIEC IT Examination Handbook requires risk assessment methodologies that reflect financial impact materiality, while CISA Cybersecurity Performance Goals emphasize quantitative risk measurement

criteria.description: Comprehensive mapping to financial services regulations including PCI-DSS, PSD2, FFIEC guidance, and NIST Cybersecurity Framework controls
criteria.pass_condition: Explicit mapping of minimum 10 threats to specific regulatory requirements with control citations from at least 3 regulatory frameworks (PCI-DSS, PSD2, FFIEC, NIST CSF) and identification of applicable compliance control categories

sub_attributes.description: Breadth of regulatory framework coverage with specific control citations
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if maps to 4+ frameworks with specific control citations, 0.7 if covers 3 frameworks with moderate detail, 0.4 if 2 frameworks with basic mapping, 0.0 if 1 or no frameworks

sub_attributes.description: Specific mapping of identified threats to regulatory control requirements
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if 15+ threats mapped to specific controls with compliance implications, 0.7 if 10-14 threats mapped, 0.4 if 5-9 threats mapped, 0.0 if <5 threats mapped

sub_attributes.description: Consideration of regulatory examination requirements and audit trail preparation
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if addresses examination procedures and audit documentation requirements, 0.7 if mentions regulatory reporting, 0.4 if basic compliance awareness, 0.0 if no examination consideration

pass_examples: OAuth token hijacking mapped to PSD2 Article 97 Strong Customer Authentication requirements and PCI-DSS requirement 8.3 for multi-factor authentication
fail_examples: Generic compliance mentions without specific regulatory citations or threat-to-control mapping
criteria.research_basis: FFIEC Cybersecurity Assessment Tool requires specific control mapping, while PSD2 RTS mandates technical security measures for payment service providers

criteria.description: Prioritized, actionable mitigation recommendations balancing security effectiveness with banking operational requirements and customer experience
criteria.pass_condition: Minimum 15 mitigations with at least 5 banking-specific controls (behavioral analytics, transaction limits, regulatory reporting, KYC integration), implementation timelines, and explicit consideration of CX impact vs security tradeoffs
pass_examples: Biometric spoofing mitigation: Multi-modal biometrics + behavioral analytics + transaction velocity limits, 6-month implementation, $2M cost vs $50M fraud prevention
fail_examples: Suggesting 'implement MFA' without considering existing banking authentication flows, regulatory requirements, or integration with core banking systems
criteria.research_basis: NIST Cybersecurity Framework 2.0 Control Implementation guidance emphasizes operational integration, while PSD2 Strong Customer Authentication requirements mandate specific implementation constraints

criteria.description: Construction of comprehensive threat matrix organizing threats by STRIDE categories, components, and attack vectors with clear risk prioritization
criteria.pass_condition: Structured threat matrix with minimum 18 threats organized by STRIDE categories (S,T,R,I,D,E) cross-referenced with application components (biometric auth, P2P payments, integrations) and clear risk priority ranking

sub_attributes.description: Comprehensive matrix structure covering all STRIDE categories and application components
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if complete matrix with all STRIDE categories and components represented, 0.7 if minor gaps in coverage, 0.4 if significant gaps, 0.0 if unstructured threat list

sub_attributes.description: Accurate categorization of threats within STRIDE framework
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if threats accurately categorized with correct STRIDE mapping, 0.7 if mostly accurate with minor misclassifications, 0.4 if several misclassifications, 0.0 if major categorization errors

sub_attributes.description: Clear prioritization methodology with consistent ranking criteria
sub_attributes.measurement: 1.0 if clear priority ranking with consistent methodology, 0.7 if ranking present with minor inconsistencies, 0.4 if basic prioritization, 0.0 if no prioritization

pass_examples: Matrix with Spoofing threats organized by component (biometric template injection for auth, OAuth impersonation for integrations) with High/Medium/Low priority rankings
fail_examples: Unstructured list of threats without clear categorization or component mapping
criteria.research_basis: Microsoft STRIDE methodology requires structured threat enumeration, while NIST Risk Management Framework emphasizes systematic threat organization

criteria.description: Evaluates whether identified threats align logically with assigned risk ratings and proposed mitigation strategies, ensuring internal consistency across threat identification, risk assessment, and mitigation planning
criteria.pass_condition: Each High/Critical risk threat (≥3 required) must have corresponding mitigation with implementation timeline ≤6 months, financial impact calculations must use consistent methodology across all threats, and no generic mitigations (e.g., 'improve security training') proposed for specific technical threats
pass_examples: Critical-rated SIM swapping threats receive specific behavioral analytics and SMS alternative mitigations with $2M investment matching $50M potential loss exposure
fail_examples: High-rated biometric template extraction threats addressed only with generic 'security awareness training' mitigation
criteria.research_basis: NIST Risk Management Framework requires logical consistency between risk identification, assessment, and treatment phases

criteria.description: Assesses whether mobile security analysis appropriately integrates with financial services threat intelligence and regulatory requirements, ensuring mobile-specific threats reflect banking operational context
criteria.pass_condition: Mobile threats must specify financial transaction impact (e.g., transaction tampering, payment fraud), financial threats must identify mobile platform dependencies (iOS/Android security features), and integration between mobile and financial analysis demonstrated through at least 3 cross-referenced threat scenarios
pass_examples: Binary tampering threats consider impact on transaction integrity and PCI compliance validation requirements for mobile payment processing
fail_examples: Mobile security analysis that ignores banking operational requirements or financial threat analysis that treats mobile as generic computing platform
criteria.research_basis: FFIEC guidance emphasizes integrated risk assessment across technology platforms and business functions
